content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660              322-5901           322-6441              322-6444

July 6, 1983


Vance Raye
Governor's Office
State Capitol
Sacramento, CA  95814

Re: Advice No. A-83-162

Dear Mr. Raye:

Thank you for your request for advice on behalf of Governor
Deukmejian. Ms. Wilman asked me to respond to your question.
You indicated during your conversation with Ms. Wilman that the
Governor attends fundraisers for various candidates. Private
air travel to and from these events is often provided. You
asked how this free transportation should be reported.

An elected state officer who receives free transportation to
another candidate's fundraiser does not have any reporting
obligation with respect to air transportation.1/ The
transportation is for political purposes, the official is not
receiving personal income which would be reportable on his or
her Statement of Economic Interests. However, even though the
official is receiving a "payment made for political purposes,"
it is not reportable as a contribution on the official's
campaign statements, because a payment is not a contribution if
"full and adequate consideration" is received in exchange for
the payment. The necessary consideration is provided by the
official's attendance at the event. (See the Cory Opinion,
1 FPPC Opinions 137 (No. 75-094-C, 1975, copy enclosed).)

1/ If the official is provided with any additional
benefits that are not incidental to the official's attendance at
the fundraiser, it is likely that such benefits will be
reportable.

Vance Raye
July 6, 1983
Page 2


     If the individual providing the transportation files
campaign reports,2/ he or she will disclose the transportation
as an in-kind contribution to the candidate for whom the
fundraiser is being given. The candidate must report the
receipt of this in-kind contribution.

     I hope that I have clarified this issue for you. If you
have any additional questions, please feel free to contact me at
(916) 322-5901.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Janis Shank McLean
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

JSM:plh

_____________________

     2/ For instance, if the individual or entity qualifies as
a major donor committee.

sections.question: You indicated during your conversation with Ms. Milman that the Governor attends fundraisers for various candidates. Private air travel to and from these events is often provided. You asked how this free transportation should be reported.
sections.conclusion: An elected state officer who receives free transportation to another candidate's fundraiser does not have any reporting obligation with respect to air transportation. The transportation is for political purposes, the official is not receiving personal income which would be reportable on his or her Statement of Economic Interests. However, even though the official is receiving a "payment made for political purposes," it is not reportable as a contribution on the official's campaign statements, because a payment is not a contribution if "full and adequate consideration" is received in exchange for the payment. The necessary consideration is provided by the official's attendance at the event.
sections.question_synthetic: How should an elected state officer report free private air transportation provided to attend another candidate's fundraiser?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Free air transportation to a fundraiser is not reportable by the receiving official on their Statement of Economic Interests, nor as a campaign contribution, because the official's attendance provides adequate consideration. However, the provider of transportation must report it as an in-kind contribution if they file campaign reports.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Clear advice letter with well-defined question and conclusion sections. References Cory Opinion (1 FPPC Opinions 137, No. 75-094-C, 1975). Two numbered footnotes provide additional context on reporting by the provider.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You indicated during your conversation with Ms. Milman that the Governor attends fundraisers for various candidates. Private air travel to and from these events is often provided. You asked how this free transportation should be reported.

CONCLUSION: An elected state officer who receives free transportation to another candidate's fundraiser does not have any reporting obligation with respect to air transportation. The transportation is for political purposes, the official is not receiving personal income which would be reportable on his or her Statement of Economic Interests. However, even though the official is receiving a "payment made for political purposes," it is not reportable as a contribution on the official's campaign statements, because a payment is not a contribution if "full and adequate consideration" is received in exchange for the payment. The necessary consideration is provided by the official's attendance at the event.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises that an elected state officer receiving free private air transportation to attend another candidate's fundraiser has no reporting obligation on their Statement of Economic Interests, and the transportation is not reportable as a campaign contribution because attendance provides adequate consideration.
